title: How Much Mortgage Can I Afford in Greater Vancouver in 2026?
metaTitle: How Much Mortgage Can I Afford in Vancouver 2026?
metaDescription: How much mortgage can a $100K, $150K or $200K household income actually qualify for in Greater Vancouver in 2026? Real numbers, stress-tested.
excerpt: Real numbers for $100K, $150K, $200K and $300K household incomes — stress-tested and Greater Vancouver-specific.
tags: how much mortgage can I afford Vancouver

body.heading: The four numbers that matter
paragraphs: Affordability comes down to: Gross Debt Service (GDS) ratio, Total Debt Service (TDS) ratio, qualifying rate, and amortization length. The federal stress test sets your qualifying rate at the higher of contract rate plus 2% or the 5.25% floor. GDS is typically capped at 39%, TDS at 44%.

body.heading: Real numbers for typical Greater Vancouver households
paragraphs: These assume: $4,500/year property tax, $1,500/year heating, $0 condo fees (detached) or $400/month (condo), $400/month other debts, 20% down payment, 25-year amortization, 4.99% contract rate, stress-tested at 6.99%.
bullets: $100,000 household income → ~$520,000 maximum mortgage → ~$650,000 maximum home (20% down)
$150,000 household income → ~$830,000 maximum mortgage → ~$1,040,000 maximum home (20% down)
$200,000 household income → ~$1,150,000 maximum mortgage → ~$1,440,000 maximum home (20% down)
$250,000 household income → ~$1,470,000 maximum mortgage → ~$1,840,000 maximum home (20% down)
$300,000 household income → ~$1,790,000 maximum mortgage → ~$2,240,000 maximum home (20% down)

body.heading: How to get more out of the same income
paragraphs: Stretch amortization to 30 years (first-time buyers and new builds): adds roughly 8–12% to your maximum. Eliminate monthly debts: every $200/month of cleared debt adds $35K–$45K of buying power. Add a co-applicant: their income gets added directly. Increase down payment to avoid CMHC premium: doesn't change qualifying directly but lowers your insured premium. Buy a property with rental income (legal suite, duplex): some lenders offset 50–80% of rental income against the new mortgage payment.

body.heading: What you should aim for vs. your maximum
paragraphs: Buying at your absolute stress-tested maximum leaves no margin for: property tax increases, rate increases at renewal, lifestyle costs, kids, surprises. Most clients I work with target 80–90% of their stress-tested maximum. The math says you can afford the bigger house. Real life often disagrees.

faqs.question: Why do my numbers come out lower than online calculators suggest?
faqs.answer: Most online calculators don't apply the stress test. The numbers above are stress-tested at 6.99%, which is what federally regulated lenders actually use to qualify you.

faqs.question: Does household income include bonuses and overtime?
faqs.answer: Bonuses and overtime are typically counted at 50% if you have 2+ years of consistent history. Without that history they're ignored. Talk to me before applying — there are workarounds for newer high-bonus situations.
